text: We present the case of a seventy four year old gentleman who presented with dysphagia and he was found to have a T three and one G junction adenocarcinoma after a new adjuvant chemo radiation therapy where he had a moderate response to chemo radiation therapy. Restaging did not reveal any metastatic disease.
Preoperatively his albumin was three point seven and hemoglobin hematocrit was twelve and thirty eight.
The video displays the placement of surgical ports on the patient's abdomen, which are used for instrument access during the esophagectomy procedure.
It's important to make sure that the ports are at least ten centimeters flat to the amyloidus as this will facilitate the mediastinal dissection as it progresses towards the neck.
The surgeon inspects the peritoneal cavity and examines the left and right hepatic lobes, observing no visible signs of gross metastases.
and the only pertinent finding was an incarcerated um, umbilical hernia, which was reduced and repaired at the end of the case.
The surgeon places a liver retractor and begins opening the thickened pars flaccida, which appears dense due to prior radiation therapy.
The surgeon maintains a lateral approach towards the caudate lobe, continuing the dissection plane towards the frontal esophageal ligament.
With G junction tumors in patients who have received neoadjuvant tumor radiation therapy, This tends to be a little bit challenging as the tissue is quite clearly fairly thickened.

The surgeon creates a retroesophageal window and passes a Penrose drain to facilitate manipulation of the gastroesophageal junction and to assist in pulling down the esophageal specimen during mediastinal dissection.
We divert attention here and we liberally use ICG Green as it can be seen. This gentleman had a BMI of thirty-two but
The surgeon identifies the right gastroepiploic vessel and its branches, which serve as the feeding vessel for the gastric conduit, visible within the surgical field.
So it's important not to injure it or grasp the fat that is on top of it which may cause spasm of the vessel and therefore it could theoretically lead to a leak.
The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to enhance visualization during the dissection, providing improved three-dimensional views of the surgical field from the console.
The surgical team alternates between using Firefly imaging and regular vision to enhance visualization during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon mixes 25 milligrams of ICG Green dye with 10 cc's of saline, administering 1.5 cc increments to enhance visualization of the vasculature during the procedure.
The surgeon ensures that the dissection remains far to the right on the screen, preserving the blood supply to the future gastric conduit.

The surgeon carefully grasps the posterior wall of the stomach, avoiding the vascular pedicle. The superior arcade is preserved to prevent vessel spasm, which could lead to ischemia and potential leakage at the gastroesophageal anastomosis site.
Again, it's important to avoid grabbing the vascular pedicle at all times.
The surgeon leaves a fat pad intact to minimize manipulation of the blood supply to the esophageal conduit, while noting the presence of short posterior vessels.
The video shows edema in the tissue, which is secondary to radiation therapy, as observed in the patient.
The surgeon begins cocorizing the duodenum, manipulating the tissue to mobilize this section of the gastrointestinal tract.
Thank you.

The gastrocolic pedicle is visible on the right side, with the groove between the duodenum and colon beginning to develop. Blunt dissection and the use of a vessel sealer aid in identifying tissue planes, with the extent of duodenal cocorization depending on the required length of the gastric conduit.
The surgeon uses ICG Green dye to assess the perfusion of the gastric conduit, with the vessels visibly illuminated, indicating good blood flow extending superiorly.
The surgeon skeletonizes the left gastric pedicle down to its base, then uses a white load stapler to simultaneously transect both the artery and the vein.
The surgeon skeletonizes the left gastric artery to perform lymphadenectomy around the gastric pedicle, ensuring thorough removal of lymph nodes as indicated by preoperative imaging.

Following the transection of the gastric pedicle, the surgeon continues transhiatal esophageal dissection. The dissection progresses through the radiated field, moving cephalad into the posterior mediastinum.
The surgeon transects part of the right crus to open the snug hiatus, preparing for further esophageal dissection.
Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.

Indocyanine green (ICG) perfusion is performed to assess the integrity of the right gastroepiploic vessel, confirming no injury. The surgeon applies the final staple line, starting with a green load and continuing with blue loads. Another ICG perfusion is conducted, showing excellent perfusion to the tip of the gastric conduit.

The surgeon removes the Penrose drain and moves the specimen, which is simultaneously pulled from the neck following completed dissection. The conduit is sutured to the specimen and carefully guided through the mediastinum to prevent rotation or twisting on the vascular pedicle. The right gastroepiploic vessel is visible, and the conduit is pulled without tension.

The post-operative upper GI study shows good flow through the conduit with no evidence of leakage in the neck. The study also reveals no pooling in the conduit and smooth transit into the first portion of the duodenum.
